data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was an ecological-commercial cascade where the exhaustion of Cypriot copper mines and the deforestation of the Levantine coast destroyed the economic basis for the entire Eastern Mediterranean trade web, leading to systemic state failure.", "mechanism": "The entire palace system was underwritten by the bronze trade, which required copper (primarily from Cyprus) and tin. By 1200 BC, the easily accessible, fuel-rich copper ores of Cyprus were depleted, requiring deeper mining and massive deforestation for smelting fuel, which in turn caused soil erosion and agricultural decline on the island. This supply shock made bronze prohibitively expensive. Simultaneously, the shipbuilding industry, which depended on Levantine cedars, collapsed due to over-logging. The loss of cheap bronze and ships dismantled the military (chariot hardware, weapons) and commercial (merchant vessels) foundations of the palaces, making them brittle and easily shattered by minor stresses.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Cyprus"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeometallurgical surveys of Cyprus show that copper production peaked in the 12th century BC, not declined, and that the island's forests were still extensive.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The widespread appearance of iron tools and weapons in the 12th century BC was a consequence of the collapse, not a cause, as ironworking technology was already known but not economically viable until bronze became scarce.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This is a deeply materialist, almost geological explanation. It elegantly explains the transition to the Iron Age not as a cause of collapse but as a technological adaptation to the collapse of the bronze supply chain. Egypt survived because it controlled its own copper and gold sources in the Sinai and Nubia and could still source tin via Red Sea routes, decoupling it from the Cypriot-Levantine axis."}
